skill.name: ebm-calculator
skill.description: Evidence-Based Medicine diagnostic test calculator. Computes sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, likelihood ratios, NNT, and pre/post-test probability from 2x2 contingency table inputs.
skill.summary: Compute key diagnostic test statistics for evidence-based medical decision making from simple numeric inputs.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 22 high-severity static findings (17 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 5 'system reconnaissance') are false positives. The scanner misidentified natural language text in audit reports and markdown files, standard library argparse calls, and JSON output format assertions as security threats. The genuine findings are low-risk: a controlled file write operation for JSON output at scripts/main.py:169, and a GitHub source attribution URL at SKILL.md:7. The 28 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are markdown code block formatting, not active code execution. The skill is a legitimate medical statistics calculator that passed 19/20 dynamic assertion tests and all security gates.

low_findings.title: File write to user-specified path
low_findings.description: Script writes JSON results to a file path provided via --output argument. This is expected CLI tool behavior controlled by the AI agent. No arbitrary path traversal risk in the skill execution context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File write is controlled by --output argument supplied by the AI agent. No exploitation vector exists within the intended skill execution model.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded source URL
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a GitHub URL linking to the original source repository for attribution. This is documentation, not a network call.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is a static markdown link for source attribution. No active network requests are made from the skill.

content.user_title: Calculate Evidence-Based Medicine Diagnostic Test Statistics
content.value_statement: Medical researchers and clinicians need accurate diagnostic test metrics but manual calculation is error-prone and time-consuming. This skill computes sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, likelihood ratios, NNT, and pre/post-test probability instantly from simple numeric inputs.
actual_capabilities: Compute sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and accuracy from 2x2 contingency table
Adjust PPV and NPV for population prevalence using Bayes theorem
Calculate Number Needed to Treat from control and experimental event rates
Convert pre-test to post-test probability using likelihood ratio
Interpret diagnostic test quality with automatic performance classification
Output results in structured JSON format to console or file
limitations: Cannot provide confidence intervals for sensitivity and specificity estimates
Does not accept raw patient-level data, only pre-aggregated contingency table values
Cannot provide clinical treatment recommendations or patient-specific medical advice
Does not handle Bayesian priors beyond simple prevalence adjustment

use_cases.title: Evaluate a New Diagnostic Test
use_cases.description: A medical researcher has validation data for a new rapid diagnostic test with 90 true positives, 10 false negatives, 85 true negatives, and 15 false positives. The skill computes sensitivity (0.90), specificity (0.85), likelihood ratios, and provides performance interpretation.
use_cases.target_user: Medical researcher evaluating diagnostic test accuracy

use_cases.title: Adjust Screening Metrics for Population Prevalence
use_cases.description: A clinician knows a diagnostic test has sample-based PPV of 0.86 but the target population has only 10% disease prevalence. The skill applies Bayes theorem to compute population-adjusted PPV (0.40) and NPV (0.99), providing realistic performance expectations.
use_cases.target_user: Clinician adapting published test metrics to local population

use_cases.title: Assess Treatment Impact with NNT
use_cases.description: A public health analyst reviewing a clinical trial with 30% control event rate and 20% experimental event rate needs the Number Needed to Treat. The skill calculates ARR (0.10), RRR (0.33), and NNT (10 patients needed to prevent one event).
use_cases.target_user: Public health analyst evaluating treatment effectiveness

prompt_templates.title: Basic Diagnostic Test Calculation
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate diagnostic test performance metrics from the following data: True Positives = 85, False Negatives = 15, True Negatives = 120, False Positives = 30. Compute sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and positive/negative likelihood ratios.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user provides a simple 2x2 contingency table for immediate EBM metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnostic Test with Prevalence Adjustment
prompt_templates.prompt: A diagnostic test has the following validation data: TP=90, FN=10, TN=80, FP=20. The disease prevalence in the target population is 8%. Calculate all EBM metrics including prevalence-adjusted PPV and NPV using Bayes theorem.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user needs realistic PPV/NPV adjusted for low-prevalence population screening.

prompt_templates.title: Number Needed to Treat (NNT) Calculation
prompt_templates.prompt: Calculate the NNT from a clinical trial where the control group had a 25% event rate and the experimental group had a 15% event rate. Provide the absolute risk reduction, relative risk reduction, and NNT with interpretation.
prompt_templates.scenario: A user has control and experimental event rates from a published clinical trial.

prompt_templates.title: Pre-Test to Post-Test Probability Conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: A patient has a pre-test probability of 20% for a condition. A diagnostic test with a likelihood ratio of 8.0 is applied. Convert the pre-test probability to post-test probability and report the probability change.
prompt_templates.scenario: A clinician uses likelihood ratio from a published study to update patient risk assessment.

output_examples.output: The diagnostic test shows sensitivity of 0.90 and specificity of 0.85. With a population prevalence of 10%, the positive predictive value is 0.40 (adjusted down from the sample-based estimate due to low prevalence). The negative predictive value is 0.99. The positive likelihood ratio is 6.0, meaning a positive result is 6 times more likely in diseased versus non-diseased patients. Overall test accuracy is 0.875.

The absolute risk reduction is 0.10, meaning the treatment reduces events by 10 percentage points compared to control. The relative risk reduction is 0.33. The Number Needed to Treat is 10, which means 10 patients must receive the treatment to prevent one additional adverse event.

Starting from a pre-test probability of 15%, the pre-test odds are 0.176. After applying the likelihood ratio of 5.2, the post-test odds become 0.918, corresponding to a post-test probability of 48%. This represents a 33 percentage point increase in disease probability.

best_practices: Always validate that the 2x2 table values (TP, FN, TN, FP) are non-negative and that prevalence is between 0 and 1 before computing metrics
When PPV/NPV from published studies need to apply to a different population, use the prevalence-adjusted mode with the target population prevalence for more realistic estimates
Document all inputs, assumptions, and the calculation mode used so results can be reproduced and verified
anti_patterns: Do not use the diagnostic mode for causal inference or to establish disease prevalence from a convenience sample
Do not apply NNT from one patient population to a different population without adjusting for baseline risk differences
Do not interpret PPV or NPV without considering the underlying disease prevalence in the target population

faq.question: What input values does the calculator need?
faq.answer: Diagnostic mode requires true positives, false negatives, true negatives, and false positives from a 2x2 contingency table. NNT mode needs control and experimental event rates. Probability mode needs pre-test probability and likelihood ratio.

faq.question: What calculation modes are available?
faq.answer: Three modes: diagnostic (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, likelihood ratios, accuracy), NNT (number needed to treat, absolute and relative risk reduction), and probability (pre-test to post-test probability conversion).

faq.question: How does prevalence adjustment work?
faq.answer: When a population prevalence is provided, the calculator uses Bayes theorem to compute PPV and NPV that reflect the actual disease rate in the target population, instead of using sample-based estimates that may be misleading.

faq.question: Can this tool replace clinical judgment?
faq.answer: No. This tool computes statistical metrics from numeric inputs. It does not provide clinical recommendations, diagnoses, or treatment decisions. Results should be interpreted by a qualified healthcare professional.

faq.question: What output format does the calculator produce?
faq.answer: Results are returned as structured text with labeled numeric values and an interpretation summary. Output can also be written to a JSON file using the --output flag for integration with other tools.

faq.question: How are edge cases handled?
faq.answer: The calculator handles division by zero by returning 0 or Infinity as appropriate. Likelihood ratios can be infinite when specificity or sensitivity is 100%. NNT is reported as Infinity when there is no treatment benefit.
